label: Hyperbolic Embeddings
definition: Representations that place data in negatively curved hyperbolic space rather than flat Euclidean space, exploiting hyperbolic volume's exponential growth to embed hierarchies and trees with low distortion.

body.text: Hyperbolic embeddings represent data in hyperbolic space, a geometry of constant negative curvature, instead of the flat Euclidean space used by ordinary embeddings. The motivation is a mismatch between the structure of much real data and the capacity of flat space. Hierarchies and trees grow exponentially: a tree where every node has b children has on the order of b raised to the depth nodes at each level. Flat Euclidean space, whose volume grows only polynomially with radius, simply does not have enough room to hold an exponentially branching structure, so embedding a deep tree into it forces distant leaves to crowd together and corrupts the distances. Hyperbolic space has volume that grows exponentially with radius, giving it exactly the room that trees need, so a hierarchy can be embedded with low distortion using strikingly few dimensions.

The most common working model is the Poincare ball, in which the entire infinite hyperbolic space is mapped into the interior of a unit ball. The metric tensor stretches dramatically as you approach the boundary, so distances near the rim are far larger than they appear, and the boundary sits at infinite distance from the center. A useful way to picture an embedded tree is to place the root near the origin and push successive levels of the hierarchy outward toward the boundary; because space expands exponentially near the rim, each level has exponentially more room than the last, and the geodesic between two leaves naturally bows inward through their common ancestor, recovering tree distance. Categories sit near the center, fine-grained instances sit near the edge, and the radial coordinate alone encodes depth in the hierarchy.

This geometry suits a wide range of machine learning data that is implicitly tree-like or scale-free. Taxonomies, ontologies, the hypernym graph of a language, social and citation networks, and the latent hierarchy of concepts in a corpus all embed more faithfully in hyperbolic than Euclidean space, often needing only two to ten dimensions to match what flat space cannot achieve at hundreds. The payoff is not only fidelity but also interpretability: the radius carries a generality-to-specificity signal for free, so the position of a point reports where it sits in the hierarchy without any extra labeling.

Working in hyperbolic space requires replacing the familiar Euclidean operations with their Riemannian manifold counterparts. Distance is the hyperbolic geodesic distance, not a straight-line or cosine distance; gradients must be converted to the manifold's tangent space and the parameters updated by Riemannian optimization so the points stay inside the ball; and addition and other vector operations are redefined through the geometry, for instance via gyrovector operations or by mapping to and from the tangent space with the exponential and logarithm maps. The cost is this added machinery and some numerical care near the boundary where the metric blows up. The benefit is a latent space whose curvature is matched to the data, a direct and practical illustration of the broader principle that choosing the right geometry, not just the right dimension, is what makes a representation faithful.

image.alt: A disk filled with a tiling of triangles that appear large at the center and shrink rapidly toward the circular boundary, never reaching it.
image.caption: Hyperbolic space, drawn here as the Poincare disk, has room that grows exponentially toward its boundary. Every triangle in this tiling is actually the same hyperbolic size; they only look smaller near the rim because distance there is far larger than it appears, and that extra room is what lets exponentially branching trees and hierarchies embed with low distortion.
